content.problemStatement:
A holiday weekend is coming up,
and Hotel Bytelandia needs to find out if it has enough rooms to accommodate all potential guests.
A number of guests have made reservations.
Each reservation consists of an arrival time, and a departure time.
The hotel management has hired you to calculate the maximum number of guests that will be at the hotel simultaneously.
Note that if one guest arrives at the same time another leaves, they are never considered to be at the hotel simultaneously
(see the second example).
constraints: departure time > arrival time for each guest
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains an integer N, followed by two lines containing N positive integers each, representing arrival and departure times.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the maximum number of guests that will be at the hotel simultaneously.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First test case: intervals (1,4),(2,5),(3,6) overlap at time 3 → 3 guests.\nSecond test case: intervals are back-to-back, never overlap → 1 guest.\nThird test case: maximum overlap occurs at times 8-9 and 13-15 with three guests simultaneously.
Only one reservation, so the maximum number of simultaneous guests is 1.
All intervals are consecutive without overlap (half-open), therefore at any moment only one guest is present.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using the two pointers technique. We first sort the arrival and departure times separately. Then, we initialize two pointers, one for arrival and one for departure, and a variable to keep track of the current number of guests. We iterate through the sorted arrival and departure times, updating the current number of guests and the maximum number of guests seen so far.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case, O(T * N log N) total. The sorting operation dominates the time complexity.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — for storing the arrival and departure times.
